ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a well-studied problem (topic coherence) but introduces a genuinely new perspective by grouping topic words into thematic "buckets" and deriving coherence from bucket properties, which is distinct from pairwise co-occurrence measures. The three proposed techniques—especially the SVD-based and SVD-Reorg methods that are parameter-free—represent a non-trivial methodological extension and are empirically shown to outperform prior state-of-the-art on two of three datasets. The authors also clearly differentiate their contributions from prior work (reported thorough related work coverage). | B(4): The paper introduces a clearly new measurement paradigm (TBuckets) that groups topic words into thematic ‘buckets’ and derives coherence from bucket properties—this is a distinct perspective compared to standard pairwise/UMass-style measures. It further contributes non-trivial new techniques (three bucket-creation methods, notably parameter-free SVD and SVD-Reorg) and empirically outperforms prior work on two of three datasets, demonstrating clear differentiation. The limitations (a clustering parameter and domain-specific word-similarity issues) indicate practical gaps but do not negate the methodological novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied problem (topic coherence) but introduces a genuinely new perspective by grouping topic words into thematic "buckets" and deriving coherence from bucket properties, which is distinct from pairwise co-occurrence measures. The three proposed techniques—especially the SVD-based and SVD-Reorg methods that are parameter-free—represent a non-trivial methodological extension and are empirically shown to outperform prior state-of-the-art on two of three datasets. The authors also clearly differentiate their contributions from prior work (reported thorough related work coverage).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new measurement paradigm (TBuckets) that groups topic words into thematic ‘buckets’ and derives coherence from bucket properties—this is a distinct perspective compared to standard pairwise/UMass-style measures. It further contributes non-trivial new techniques (three bucket-creation methods, notably parameter-free SVD and SVD-Reorg) and empirically outperforms prior work on two of three datasets, demonstrating clear differentiation. The limitations (a clustering parameter and domain-specific word-similarity issues) indicate practical gaps but do not negate the methodological novelty.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates TBuckets on three datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) and compares to the state-of-the-art reported in Röder et al. (2015), showing TBuckets-SVD-Reorg outperforms on 2/3 datasets and demonstrating additional utility for weakly supervised classification. It presents three method variants and reports hyperparameters, and the related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not indicate detailed ablation or sensitivity analyses beyond comparing the three variants, code is not mentioned (limiting reproducibility), and some limitations are only briefly noted (clustering parameter and domain-specific word similarity), so experiments are solid but with minor gaps. | B(4): Experiments are reasonably comprehensive: methods are evaluated on three datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) and compared to state-of-the-art (Röder et al. 2015), with TBuckets-SVD-Reorg outperforming on 2 of 3 datasets and Pearson r reported. Related work coverage is said to be thorough and the paper discusses limitations (clustering parameter and domain-specific word similarity issues); hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not indicate dedicated ablation or sensitivity analyses for the proposed components (e.g., effect of EVS weighting or bucket reorganization), nor is code availability mentioned, which limits full reproducibility—hence a deduction of one point.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates TBuckets on three datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) and compares to the state-of-the-art reported in Röder et al. APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): This paper addresses topic coherence measurement for topic models, a core NLP/ML task, and proposes novel, practical methods (TBuckets variants using embeddings, clustering, and SVD). It evaluates against the state-of-the-art (Röder et al. 2015) on standard datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) using appropriate metrics (Pearson r with human judgments) and reports hyperparameters and limitations. The methodological choices and evaluation align with expectations for a top NLP/ML venue and the work appears targeted to the right audience (topic-modeling and evaluation researchers). | B(4): The paper addresses topic coherence measurement for topic models — a well-established evaluation and modelling concern in the NLP/ML community — and proposes TBuckets with concrete methods (clustering and SVD variants) that are evaluated against prior art (Röder et al. 2015) on standard datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) using accepted metrics (Pearson r). The inclusion of parameter-free SVD methods, comparison to state-of-the-art baselines, and applicability to weakly supervised text classification make it relevant to a top-tier NLP/ML audience. Minor scope concern: the work is primarily an evaluation/metric contribution (rather than a large new learning model), so it may be a slightly better fit for NLP venues focused on evaluation or applied ML tracks rather than flagship broad-theory tracks.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses topic coherence measurement for topic models, a core NLP/ML task, and proposes novel, practical methods (TBuckets variants using embeddings, clustering, and SVD). The methodological choices and evaluation align with expectations for a top NLP/ML venue and the work appears targeted to the right audience (topic-modeling and evaluation researchers).

The inclusion of parameter-free SVD methods, comparison to state-of-the-art baselines, and applicability to weakly supervised text classification make it relevant to a top-tier NLP/ML audience. IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of automatically measuring topic quality and introduces a novel, interpretable framing (grouping topic words into thematic buckets) that is conceptually distinct from prior coherence measures. The SVD-based TBuckets variants are parameter-free and empirically outperform the state-of-the-art reported in Röder et al. (2015) on two of three datasets (NYT and 20NG), and the method shows additional utility for weakly supervised text classification, suggesting practical uptake. However, mixed results on the Genomics dataset and noted domain-specific word-similarity limitations indicate the approach is promising but not universally solved, so its impact is strong but not yet transformative. | B(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—automatic measurement of topic coherence—and introduces a novel, interpretable ‘bucket’ formulation using word embeddings and SVD/clustering. Empirically TBuckets (notably TBuckets-SVD-Reorg) outperforms the prior state-of-the-art on 2 of 3 datasets (NYT and 20NG) and the SVD-based variants are parameter-free, which increases their practical appeal. The approach also demonstrates utility beyond evaluation (weakly supervised text classification), suggesting the idea could be adopted and extended by others. Remaining domain sensitivity (Genomics) and a clustering parameter for one variant temper claims of universal applicability but do not negate likely significant uptake in the topic-modeling community.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of automatically measuring topic quality and introduces a novel, interpretable framing (grouping topic words into thematic buckets) that is conceptually distinct from prior coherence measures. The SVD-based TBuckets variants are parameter-free and empirically outperform the state-of-the-art reported in Röder et al. (2015) on two of three datasets (NYT and 20NG), and the method shows additional utility for weakly supervised text classification, suggesting practical uptake. However, mixed results on the Genomics dataset and noted domain-specific word-similarity limitations indicate the approach is promising but not universally solved, so its impact is strong but not yet transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—automatic measurement of topic coherence—and introduces a novel, interpretable ‘bucket’ formulation using word embeddings and SVD/clustering. Empirically TBuckets (notably TBuckets-SVD-Reorg) outperforms the prior state-of-the-art on 2 of 3 datasets (NYT and 20NG) and the SVD-based variants are parameter-free, which increases their practical appeal. The approach also demonstrates utility beyond evaluation (weakly supervised text classification), suggesting the idea could be adopted and extended by others. Remaining domain sensitivity (Genomics) and a clustering parameter for one variant temper claims of universal applicability but do not negate likely significant uptake in the topic-modeling community.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper reports hyperparameters (e.g., TBuckets-Clustering 'Max Dist') and specifies the main algorithmic components (use of pre-trained embeddings, clustering/SVD variants and coherence scores), and evaluation uses Pearson's r which is clearly defined. However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary, code is not mentioned, and the Genomics domain/word-similarity details are noted as a limitation, which hampers exact reproduction. Datasets are named (20NG, NYT, Genomics) but not fully described here, so reimplementation is possible but would require filling in several important experimental details. | B(3): The paper reports hyperparameters and model configurations (hyperparameters reported: True) and uses a precise evaluation metric (Pearson's r against human judgments). However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not described in the provided summary, code availability is not mentioned, and one dataset (Genomics) may require domain-specific handling—making reimplementation dependent on unstated choices. Datasets 20NG and NYT are standard/public but reproducibility would still require the missing preprocessing and code details, so only partial reproduction is feasible.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters (e.g., TBuckets-Clustering 'Max Dist') and specifies the main algorithmic components (use of pre-trained embeddings, clustering/SVD variants and coherence scores), and evaluation uses Pearson's r which is clearly defined. However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary, code is not mentioned, and the Genomics domain/word-similarity details are noted as a limitation, which hampers exact reproduction. Datasets are named (20NG, NYT, Genomics) but not fully described here, so reimplementation is possible but would require filling in several important experimental details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and model configurations (hyperparameters reported: True) and uses a precise evaluation metric (Pearson's r against human judgments). However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not described in the provided summary, code availability is not mentioned, and one dataset (Genomics) may require domain-specific handling—making reimplementation dependent on unstated choices. Datasets 20NG and NYT are standard/public but reproducibility would still require the missing preprocessing and code details, so only partial reproduction is feasible.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence: TBuckets variants (especially TBuckets-SVD-Reorg) outperform prior methods on 2 of 3 datasets and TBuckets overall outperforms baselines in the authors' experiments. Mathematical content appears limited to algorithmic descriptions (SVD/clustering) so no derivation issues are evident from the summary. However, the summary does not state whether baselines were re-run under identical conditions or whether statistical significance tests were performed, and code availability is not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility. Given these minor methodological gaps (and the admitted domain-specific limitations on Genomics), the technical contribution appears sound but not flawless. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments: TBuckets variants (notably TBuckets‑SVD‑Reorg) outperform the prior state‑of‑the‑art on 2 of 3 datasets (NYT and 20NG) and TBuckets overall shows better correlations with human judgements (Pearson’s r) in the reported experiments. Mathematical components (use of SVD, clustering, eigenvector scores) are standard and there are no evident derivation errors in the summary. However, the methodology has minor weaknesses: baselines are compared “as reported” from Röder et al. (2015) and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, and domain sensitivity (Genomics) is acknowledged but not fully addressed. Given these limited methodological gaps, the technical claims are credible but not flawless.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence: TBuckets variants (especially TBuckets-SVD-Reorg) outperform prior methods on 2 of 3 datasets and TBuckets overall outperforms baselines in the authors' experiments. Mathematical content appears limited to algorithmic descriptions (SVD/clustering) so no derivation issues are evident from the summary. However, the summary does not state whether baselines were re-run under identical conditions or whether statistical significance tests were performed, and code availability is not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility. Given these minor methodological gaps (and the admitted domain-specific limitations on Genomics), the technical contribution appears sound but not flawless.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments: TBuckets variants (notably TBuckets‑SVD‑Reorg) outperform the prior state‑of‑the‑art on 2 of 3 datasets (NYT and 20NG) and TBuckets overall shows better correlations with human judgements (Pearson’s r) in the reported experiments. Mathematical components (use of SVD, clustering, eigenvector scores) are standard and there are no evident derivation errors in the summary. However, the methodology has minor weaknesses: baselines are compared “as reported” from Röder et al. (2015) and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, and domain sensitivity (Genomics) is acknowledged but not fully addressed. Given these limited methodological gaps, the technical claims are credible but not flawless.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper compares against state-of-the-art coherence measures as reported in Röder et al. (2015) and evaluates on the same datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) using Pearson's r, indicating fair, like-for-like comparisons. It reports per-dataset results (TBuckets-SVD-Reorg outperforms on 2 of 3 datasets) and explicitly notes limitations on the Genomics dataset and word-similarity choices, showing acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably/better. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper analyzes differences (parameter-free SVD variants, bucket-based scores), supporting comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant baselines. | B(4): The paper compares against the state-of-the-art baseline from Röder et al. (2015) on three standard datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) using the same evaluation metric (Pearson's r), and reports hyperparameters and thorough related-work coverage. Comparisons appear fair and conducted under the same conditions, and the authors analyze differences (e.g., bucket properties, parameter-free SVD variants) and acknowledge that TBuckets does not beat prior methods on the Genomics dataset and that domain-specific word similarities are a limitation. Given this, the experimental comparison is strong but not perfectly comprehensive (minor omissions such as explicit code release or additional recent baselines are not mentioned).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against state-of-the-art coherence measures as reported in Röder et al. It reports per-dataset results (TBuckets-SVD-Reorg outperforms on 2 of 3 datasets) and explicitly notes limitations on the Genomics dataset and word-similarity choices, showing acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably/better. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper analyzes differences (parameter-free SVD variants, bucket-based scores), supporting comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant baselines.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized and presents its main contributions (the TBuckets family, including TBuckets-Clustering, TBuckets-SVD, and TBuckets-SVD-Reorg) early and clearly, with a coherent methodology and experiments on 20NG, NYT, and Genomics. Notation and methods (WL, EVS, use of word embeddings and SVD) are described sufficiently for replication, hyperparameters are reported, and related work coverage is thorough; limitations are explicitly acknowledged. Minor presentation gaps remain (the summary does not indicate the effectiveness of figures/tables or availability of code), so I rate it as well-written with only minor presentation issues. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear progression from method to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (the three TBuckets techniques and the SVD-Reorg claim) are stated up front. Methods are described clearly (agglomerative clustering, SVD, SVD-Reorg) and how coherence is computed is explicit; hyperparameters are reported and dataset/evaluation choices (Pearson's r on 20NG, NYT, Genomics) are transparent. Notation and presentation appear consistent and the writing quality is described as good with thorough related work coverage. Minor presentation issues remain (e.g., one technique requires a tuning parameter and domain-specific similarity limitations are noted), but these are acknowledged in the text rather than obscuring the narrative.

main_claims: Proposes TBuckets, a novel approach that groups topic words into thematic 'buckets' and uses bucket properties to measure LDA topic coherence.
Presents three bucket-creation techniques: TBuckets-Clustering (agglomerative clustering), TBuckets-SVD (singular value decomposition), and TBuckets-SVD-Reorg (SVD with reorganization).

Shows TBuckets-SVD-Reorg outperforms the state-of-the-art (as reported in Röder et al. (2015)) on 2 of 3 evaluated datasets (NYT and 20NG), and TBuckets overall outperforms prior methods in their experiments.
Highlights that the SVD-based TBuckets techniques are parameter-free and demonstrates utility of TBuckets for weakly supervised text classification.
_summary.methodology: Represent topic words using pre-trained word embeddings and group them into 'buckets' via (i) agglomerative clustering, (ii) SVD to discover sub-themes, or (iii) SVD with bucket reorganization; compute topic coherence from properties of the largest bucket (size), an LDA order-weighted sum (WL), and for SVD methods an eigenvector-similarity score (EVS) with SVD scores weighted by the square of the highest singular value.
baselines: State-of-the-art methods as reported in Röder et al. (2015)
metrics: Pearson's r correlation coefficient (between human judgements and automatic coherence scores)
limitations: TBuckets-Clustering requires one parameter ('Max Dist' for agglomerative merging).
Current word similarity computations may not be optimal for specific domains (e.g., Genomics); authors plan to devise better domain-suitable word similarity methods.
